title: The State of Open Data in the Caribbean: A 2023 Retrospective
excerpt: A comparative analysis of Spatial Data Infrastructure (SDI) implementations across CARICOM nations.
content:
Open data is a policy choice. It requires governments to decide that the information they collect at public expense — land registries, census records, infrastructure inventories, environmental monitoring data — should be accessible to the citizens and researchers who might use it, rather than managed as an administrative asset to be released selectively or not at all. In 2023, GuyNode conducted a systematic review of national Spatial Data Infrastructure (SDI) implementations across the Caribbean Community (CARICOM). What we found is a region characterized by significant institutional variation, a handful of genuine successes, and a persistent gap between policy commitment and operational reality.

Methodology

The review assessed 12 CARICOM member states against five dimensions: accessibility (can data be found and downloaded without registration or fee?), licensing (are terms of use clearly stated and permissive?), currency (how recently was key administrative data updated?), spatial coverage (do datasets cover the full national territory or only selected areas?), and machine readability (are data available in formats that can be processed without manual intervention?). Each dimension was scored on a three-point scale — inadequate, developing, mature — based on direct inspection of national data portals and SDI nodes, supplemented by interviews with national GIS coordinators where these could be arranged.

The review explicitly excluded privately-held datasets and data held by international organizations. The focus was on nationally-produced, nationally-held spatial data that member state governments had either committed to releasing openly or that was already available through official channels.

The Leaders: Jamaica and Trinidad and Tobago

Jamaica's National Spatial Data Management Division (NSDMD) operates what is, by Caribbean standards, a mature open data infrastructure. The Jamaica SDI portal provides open access to administrative boundaries at the parish, constituency, and community levels; land use classifications from the National Environment and Planning Agency; infrastructure datasets including roads, schools, and health facilities; and a regularly updated population grid derived from census data. Licensing terms are clearly stated under a Creative Commons Attribution framework. Data is available in both Shapefile and GeoJSON formats. Update cycles for core administrative layers are annual at minimum.

Trinidad and Tobago presents a more mixed picture. The Town and Country Planning Division and the Central Statistical Office both maintain data portals with significant holdings, but coordination between them is limited and the licensing terms on several key datasets are ambiguous — a common situation where data was placed online without a deliberate licensing decision, leaving users uncertain about permissibility for commercial or derivative uses. The quality of the geospatial data itself is high; the governance layer around it is still developing.

The Middle Tier: Barbados, Belize, and Guyana

Barbados, Belize, and Guyana all fall into a middle tier characterized by substantial data holdings, partial portal infrastructure, and inconsistent update cycles. Each has a national mapping agency with significant cadastral and topographic data, but the path from that data to a publicly accessible, machine-readable download is non-trivial in all three cases.

Guyana's situation is instructive. The Guyana Lands and Surveys Commission (GLSC) holds the most comprehensive national spatial datasets in the country — cadastral parcels, topographic coverage, administrative boundaries, and hydrographic data. Some of this data is available for purchase or on request; some is publicly available in PDF or scanned map format; very little is available as open, machine-readable vector data. GuyNode's catalog exists precisely to fill this gap — digitizing, georeferencing, and standardizing data from the GLSC and other national agencies and making it available under open terms. But the existence of GuyNode as a civil-society initiative highlights the absence of an equivalent official effort, not a replacement for one.

Belize stands out within this tier for the quality of its open environmental data, driven in part by the requirements of international conservation finance instruments that mandate data transparency as a condition of funding. The institutional capacity built to satisfy those requirements has created spillover benefits for broader spatial data availability.

Constraints Across the Region

Several constraints recur across jurisdictions regardless of income level or institutional capacity:

Licensing uncertainty is pervasive. Data placed online without an explicit license is not, in practice, open data — a user cannot legally reuse it without seeking permission, which recreates the friction that open data is meant to eliminate. This is a solvable problem that requires administrative decisions, not technical ones. The most common barrier is not that agencies refuse to license data openly; it is that the question of licensing has never been formally considered.

Format fragmentation remains a serious obstacle. The same dataset may be available as a scanned PDF of a printed map, a proprietary CAD file, a Shapefile with a non-standard projection and missing attribute documentation, and an ArcGIS REST service — in different repositories, with no indication of which is current. Machine readability requires not just an available format but a format that is documented, consistently structured, and updated in step with the underlying data.

Update frequency is the dimension on which even the more mature SDIs most frequently fall short. A boundary layer that was accurate in 2015 is not open data in any meaningful sense in 2023 if boundary changes since then are not reflected. For administrative boundaries in particular — which are the foundation on which most other spatial analyses depend — staleness is a fundamental quality problem.

The Role of Civil Society and Regional Coordination

Two developments outside formal government SDI programs deserve recognition. The first is the emergence of civil society data initiatives — GuyNode in Guyana, and analogous efforts in several other CARICOM states — that are filling gaps left by official portals through digitization, standardization, and open publication. These initiatives are valuable but fragile; they depend on volunteer labor and donor funding that is not guaranteed beyond project cycles.

The second is the work of the Caribbean Community Climate Change Centre (CCCCC) and the Caribbean Disaster Emergency Management Agency (CDEMA), both of which have invested in regional spatial data infrastructure for climate and disaster risk management. The datasets produced through these programs — particularly regional exposure data and hazard footprints — represent the closest thing to coordinated regional spatial data governance that currently exists in CARICOM. Extending this model beyond the climate and disaster domain would require political commitment that is not yet visible in regional data governance discussions.

Recommendations

Three actions would have the highest leverage on the open data landscape across the region. First, adoption of a standard open data license — Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 is the obvious choice — as the default for all nationally-produced spatial data, with derogations requiring explicit justification. Second, investment in API-based data delivery infrastructure that eliminates the need for manual download and re-hosting, which is currently how most data reuse happens in the region. Third, a regional data standards body — even an informal technical working group operating under the Caribbean Meteorological Organisation or CCCCC umbrella — that could establish common schema and coordinate standards and reduce the fragmentation that currently makes cross-border spatial analysis unnecessarily difficult.

None of these recommendations requires large capital investment. They require institutional will and coordination — which, in the Caribbean context, is the genuinely scarce resource.
